did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks) and a genetic algorithm to automatically discover and train a multimodal fusion architecture for the multiclass diagnosis of skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) using the ISIC2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design of a genetic algorithm to discover multi-view deep neural network architectures for skin lesion diagnosis. It details the methodology, dataset (ISIC2019), experimental setup, and results (recall scores for malignant lesions), which constitutes a technical study producing new results rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Multi-view Deep Neural Networks for multiclass skin lesion diagnosis
journal: 2022 IEEE International Conference on Omni-layer Intelligent Systems (COINS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the multiclass diagnosis of skin lesions by discovering a multimodal fusion architecture that combines medical images and clinical meta-data. The authors designed a genetic algorithm to automatically select and optimize the architecture's structure, including feed-forward settings and CNN output features, using the ISIC2019 dataset. The experimental evaluation involved training models on 72,106 images and meta-data across eight skin lesion categories using a 3-times 10-fold cross-validation approach. The proposed joint fusion architecture outperformed the baseline MobileNet model, achieving a higher Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.73 compared to the baseline's 0.68. Specifically, the model demonstrated strong diagnostic capabilities for malignant lesions, with recall scores of 85% for melanoma, 94% for basal cell carcinoma, and 84% for squamous cell carcinoma. The results confirm the suitability of using a genetic algorithm to build effective multimodal fusion architectures for skin lesion diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Eight skin lesion categories (nevus, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using recall scores for specific classes (e.g., 85% for melanoma, 94% for basal cell carcinoma, 84% for squamous cell carcinoma) and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC).

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 dataset, obtained from the ISIC repository. It comprises dermoscopic images and clinical meta-data (sex, age, anatomical site) for skin lesion diagnosis. Instances with missing meta-data were removed.

use.training: Used for training the multi-view deep neural network architectures. Training data was augmented (rotations, flips, crops) and balanced by creating new images until the ratio matched the largest class. Meta-data was included as input.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. Test data kept the original ratio between categories and was not augmented. Evaluated using a 3-times 10-fold cross-validation process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the effectiveness of each model and average results across all fold executions.
To measure the predictive performance of the models in each fold and as a fitness function for the genetic algorithm; used to compare the proposed model against the baseline.
To evaluate diagnostic capabilities for specific classes, particularly malignant lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma).
To rank and aggregate the performance of different architecture types (early, joint, late fusion) discovered by the genetic algorithm.
To visualize and analyze the classification performance and errors for the baseline and proposed architectures.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using recall scores, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and Borda scores for ranking architectures.

ISIC2019.notes: The proposed joint fusion architecture outperformed the baseline MobileNet model. The baseline achieved an MCC of 0.68, while the proposed model achieved an MCC of 0.73. The proposed model identified 18 more malignant cases than the baseline and improved predictive capability for all categories except dermatofibroma (where performance was tied). Vascular lesion, basal c.c., and dermatofibroma were the most clearly identifiable categories with recall scores of 96%, 94%, and 91% respectively in the best performing fold/aggregate context mentioned in text, though Table III shows specific fold values. The best individual architecture was encoded as [1,5,8,1,0.6,16,1,128,0]. Borda scores for architecture types: Late fusion (1875), Joint fusion (1600), Early fusion (738).

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-view Deep Neural Network (discovered via Genetic Algorithm) and Baseline MobileNet
architecture_used.pretraining: None (Transfer learning was not used; weights initialized using Kaiming method)
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 (height, width, channels) for images; Sex, Age, Anatomical Site for meta-data

architecture_details.proposed: Joint Fusion Architecture discovered by Genetic Algorithm. Encoded as [1,5,8,1,0.6,16,1,128,0]. Combines CNN output features (abstract features) from image view and Feed Forward Network output from meta-data view before passing to a final predictive model. Shares network parameters implicitly to mitigate overfitting.

training_details.loss_function: Average of categorical cross-entropy along all training samples
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced by a factor of 0.1 if no improvement in predictive performance observed during 10 epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotation (1°-270°), random flips (X/Y-axis), random cropping (10%-30%)

genetic_algorithm_settings.elitism: Generational elitism (best parent replaces worst child)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multi-view Deep Neural Network (discovered via Genetic Algorithm)
preprocessing_techniques: Data filtering: Removal of 2,556 instances with missing meta-data attributes from the ISIC2019 dataset.
Data balancing: Training data was balanced by creating new images until the ratio matched the largest class.
Data augmentation (Training): Random rotation (1°-270°), random flips (X-axis and Y-axis), and random cropping (10%-30%).
Test data handling: Test data was not augmented to keep the original ratio between categories.
Meta-data integration: Clinical meta-data (sex, age, anatomical site) was included as input alongside images.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Angles between 1° and 270° were applied to training images.
Flips along the X-axis and Y-axis were applied to training images.
Crops between 10% and 30% were applied to training images.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class Balancing via Synthetic Image Generation
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Training data was balanced by creating new images until the ratio matched the largest class; these new images were considered independent from the original ones.

conclusions: The proposed multi-view deep neural network architecture, discovered via a genetic algorithm, outperformed the baseline MobileNet model in multiclass skin lesion diagnosis, achieving a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.73 compared to the baseline's 0.68.

The discovered joint fusion architecture demonstrated strong diagnostic capabilities for malignant lesions, achieving recall scores of 85% for melanoma, 94% for basal cell carcinoma, and 84% for squamous cell carcinoma.

The model identified 18 more malignant cases and a total of 74 more accurately identified lesions across all categories compared to the baseline, with performance improvements in all categories except dermatofibroma (where performance was tied).
Vascular lesions, basal cell carcinoma, and dermatofibroma were identified as the most clearly identifiable categories, with recall scores of 96%, 94%, and 91% respectively.
The study confirms the suitability of using a genetic algorithm to automatically build effective multimodal fusion architectures for skin lesion diagnosis.
Processing clinical meta-data through a feed-forward network before fusion proved more useful than raw meta-data integration, highlighting the value of abstract feature extraction from meta-data.

Among the fusion types evaluated (early, joint, late), the joint fusion architecture achieved the best overall performance, while late fusion ranked highest in Borda score aggregation but was not the best individual architecture.
The inclusion of a feed-forward network for meta-data processing did not significantly increase the training time per epoch (approx. 271-277 seconds for 72,106 images).

Future work should include more state-of-the-art CNN models and datasets to validate the genetic algorithm's performance, analyze the impact of GA hyperparameters, and explore custom crossover methods.
The approach shows potential for application on non-dermoscopic images and embedding on mobile devices to boost preventive and economical medical services.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC2019 dataset, which may limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices; no external validation was performed.
Training data was artificially balanced by generating synthetic images to match the largest class, which may introduce bias or overfitting risks not present in real-world clinical distributions.
Test data was not augmented and retained the original class imbalance, providing a more realistic assessment of performance on imbalanced clinical data.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance differences were assessed using Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) and recall scores.
The genetic algorithm search space was limited to specific architectural components (e.g., number of hidden layers, optimizer choice), potentially missing other optimal configurations.
Transfer learning was explicitly not used to isolate the effect of data augmentation and architecture discovery, which may reduce performance compared to models leveraging pre-trained weights.
The model's performance on 'nevus' (the most complex and common class) was lower than other categories, indicating potential difficulty in distinguishing benign nevi from other lesions.
Computational resources were substantial (4x RTX 2080-Ti, 4x RTX 1080-Ti, 2x GTX 780), which may hinder reproducibility or deployment in resource-constrained settings.

The paper acknowledges that the genetic algorithm's performance is sensitive to hyperparameters and suggests future work on custom crossover methods, indicating current results may not be fully optimized.

Clinical meta-data (sex, age, anatomical site) was processed through a feed-forward network before fusion, which was found to be more effective than raw meta-data integration, highlighting the importance of feature extraction for non-image data.
